data.title: India overtakes Japan in economic size, but prosperity trails far behind
data.description:
India ushered in the new year with a symbolic triumph as its economy edged past Japan’s to become the world’s fourth largest by nominal gross domestic product.
The achievement unleashed a wave of national pride in New Delhi and among business leaders, but behind the celebrations lingers a question: can the world’s fastest-growing economy turn rapid growth into widespread prosperity?
Economists warn that GDP figures alone cannot create jobs, lift incomes or bridge yawning inequality in a nation of more than 1.4 billion people.
The challenge now is maintaining momentum and continuing to drive reforms of taxes, labour rules and foreign investment norms to turn statistical success into tangible gains for its young, still-poor population.

For Kunal Gupta, these reforms have unfolded on the factory floor. As CEO of electric-bike maker EMotorad, he has seen a fundamental shift in how businesses approach manufacturing.
“We are constantly seeing people trying to get into manufacturing under the Make in India campaign. Businesses are taking the harder step to localisation,” he said, referencing a government drive to boost a sector that still punches below its weight compared to China and other regional competitors such as Vietnam and Thailand.
India’s recent overhaul of its goods and services tax and introduction of simplified labour laws after years of delays should help improve the business climate, Gupta said.
His company, once reliant on imported parts, opened a manufacturing plant in Pune, Maharashtra state, to produce batteries and chargers locally, helped by the government’s Production Linked Incentive scheme introduced during the pandemic to reward increased domestic output.
But he says demand for his e-bikes has been blunted by a dearth of export incentives, high logistics costs compared to regional rivals and limited access to credit, despite the spread of digital finance through the government-backed Unified Payments Interface.
Faster growth needed
Analysts warn that India’s demographic advantage – more than a quarter of its people are aged between 10 and 26 – could become a liability if growth fails to create well-paid jobs.
The country must create at least 8 million jobs a year over the next decade to realise its goal of becoming a developed nation by 2047, according to the government’s chief economic adviser V. Anantha Nageswaran, who has also called for manufacturing’s share of GDP to be lifted.
“The biggest challenge, apart from India’s size, is that the external environment will not be as benign over the next 10 to 20 years,” he said last year at Columbia University’s India Summit 2025.
Despite projections that India will remain the fastest-growing G20 economy, its per capita GDP of around US$2,700 in 2024 was roughly 12 times lower than Japan’s and 20 times below Germany’s.
India needs to grow faster for longer
N.R. Bhanumurthy, economist
“India needs to grow faster for longer,” said N.R. Bhanumurthy, director of the Chennai-based Madras School of Economics, adding that “potential growth” should be “nothing less than 7.5 per cent”.
The World Bank projects growth of 6.5 per cent this year while Moody’s forecasts a similar pace through at least 2027.
Those numbers could help India move towards its goal of becoming the world’s third-largest economy by 2030 – and a “developed nation” by the centenary of its independence in 2047 – but only if reforms continue and productivity rises.
External pressures have sharpened India’s reform focus, especially since Washington imposed import tariffs as high as 50 per cent last year.

These changes include Delhi’s recent rationalisation of the goods and services tax – cutting the top rate for some goods from 28 to 18 per cent – and new labour laws that make hiring and firing easier.
Prime Minister Narendra Modi’s government has also fully opened up the insurance sector to foreign investors, removing previous investment caps, and is reviewing customs rules that currently penalise domestic manufacturers by taxing inputs more heavily than finished goods.
“Many more reforms are in the pipeline,” Bhanumurthy said. “All should help [with growth].”
Multinational companies have taken notice. Financial incentives introduced since the pandemic have drawn major manufacturers such as Apple and its suppliers, which have ramped up iPhone production in India.
Yet manufacturing still contributes only about 16 to 17 per cent of GDP, lagging behind the East Asian average of 20 to 25 per cent.
“The biggest challenges are accelerating private investments, including foreign investments; maintaining the growth in consumption; and making sure that more people get decent jobs,” Amitendu Palit, a senior research fellow at the National University of Singapore’s Institute of South Asian Studies, told This Week in Asia.
India’s currency, the rupee, hit a record low against the US dollar late last year, cushioning exporters against US tariffs.
The South Asian nation’s large domestic market and expanding ties with Europe, China and the Gulf should further limit the fallout from Washington’s trade measures, say analysts like Jamus Lim, an associate professor of economics at ESSEC Business School.
Fixing the foundations
Agriculture is India’s largest source of employment but lags in productivity. Nearly half of all workers depend on it, yet crop yields are often only around half those in more advanced economies.
Biswajit Dhar of the Council for Social Development said stronger agricultural research and innovation were essential.
“Why can’t the government strengthen existing agricultural research institutions and encourage them to produce more high-yielding varieties and help farmers get more yield?” he asked, citing the success of the “Green Revolution” of the 1960s that made India self-sufficient in grain production.
Dhar also pointed to the “White Revolution”, a dairy cooperative initiative that turned India into the world’s largest producer of milk, as proof of how inclusive models can transform rural livelihoods.

Boosting earnings from agriculture would ripple through the entire economy, Dhar said.
“If we put more money in the hands of people, then this demand problem will disappear,” he said, stressing that more needed to be done about “huge inequality” in the country.
“Poorer sections [of society] are not going to be standing on the sidelines and let people get richer.”
Economists stress that growth alone cannot bridge the inequality gap without deep investment in health, education and human capital. Studies show wide gaps between rural and urban access to quality schooling and healthcare, even as government schemes make incremental progress.
Medical insurance providing up to 500,000 rupees (US$5,600) per family for hospitalisation and tertiary care has narrowed disparities, but a yawning gulf remains, particularly in rural districts, according to The Lancet’s annual Global Burden of Disease study.
Vast variation also persists in school quality between provinces, despite improvements made through initiatives such as the New Education Policy and the Sarva Shiksha Abhiyan (Education for All Movement) programme, aimed at universal elementary education.

India “has to continue improving basic educational attainment for its large working-age population, and better integrate this workforce into global supply chains”, said Lim of ESSEC Business School.
He also called for greater support for small and medium-sized enterprises, lower capital costs and a stable rupee.
Sushant Sarin, head of strategy and commercial risk solutions for India at professional services firm Aon, said Delhi must strengthen supply chain resilience against disruptions “linked to climate impact, logistics bottlenecks and infrastructure gaps”.
He added that cyber readiness and technology governance would also become vital to competitiveness as industries digitalise.
Despite the obstacles, India’s business climate is changing in ways that executives find tangible.
A continuous focus on execution would definitely unlock more value
Sarvadnya Kulkarni, Indian manufacturing executive
Sarvadnya Kulkarni, CEO of General Instruments Consortium, a leading manufacturer of industrial gauges and sensors, credited recent labour and logistics reforms with removing friction from everyday operations.
“A continuous focus on execution would definitely unlock more value,” he told This Week in Asia. “But I would say the systems that have been put in place are really good.”
Dr Satya Ramani Vadlamani, founder of pharmaceutical products manufacturer Murli Krishna Pharma, said the “bureaucratic maze” that existed two decades ago had become far simpler to navigate.
Simplified tax procedures, for instance, now mean refunds are processed within a month – something that would have previously been unimaginable.
“We can’t expect things to change overnight,” she added. “We still have problems dealing with bureaucracy, but at least things are changing.”

data.title: Women’s Foil World Cup: Champion Liu keeping expectations in check, 1 city fencer competing
data.description:
Defending fencing champion Jaelyn Liu called Hong Kong her “lucky place” ahead of her title defence on Saturday at the FIE Women’s Foil World Cup, while Daphne Chan Nok-sze is the city’s only representative to make it to the round of 64.
Since her triumph in Hong Kong a year ago, the 16-year-old American has established herself as one of the brightest rising stars in fencing.
Liu captured both the senior and junior world titles in 2025, becoming the first and youngest American to win a world championship in foil. She has moved up the rankings from No 93 to No 13 within a year.
Still, Liu acknowledged she had a “rough” start to her 2026 season and she was trying to keep her expectations in check.
“I haven’t done well in the past two competitions, so I just want to go out there, fence my best and fight as I’ve never won anything before,” she said.
“I think that is part of learning. I want to slow down a bit and focus on improvement rather than just winning every tournament.
“My long-term goal is to become an Olympic champion, so I want to build my game step by step, especially my offence. I don’t want to rely on lucky 15-14 wins – I want to dominate the strip and show my skill level.”

Liu said she enjoys spending time in the city where she claimed her first World Cup title last year.
“I definitely believe Hong Kong is one of my lucky places,” Liu said. “Mostly because I did really well here last year – but I hope it’s not just luck. I find a lot of energy here; I know the people and I am comfortable with my surroundings. I want to use that to power up for tomorrow [Saturday].”
Liu will face China’s Zhang Siqi in the round of 64.
Out of the 198 entries, the top 16 fencers in the world earned byes into the round of 64, with the remaining competitors battling through pools and direct elimination bouts for the remaining slots.
For Hong Kong, Chan was the only one to advance. The world No 33 edged through qualification after winning four of her six pool bouts, a contrast to last year when she swept all six to gain direct entry.
Chan defeated teammate Wu Jiawei 15-4 in the round of 128, then overcame China’s Shi Yue to secure her spot in Saturday’s main draw.
Chan next faces Olympic silver medallist Lauren Scruggs of the United States.
The Hong Kong fencer declined post-match interviews, saying she preferred to “stay focused until after the competition concludes”.
Teammate Sophia Wu, who is ranked No 65 and reached the main draw last year, suffered a narrow 15-14 defeat to Japan’s Shiori Takezawa in the first round of elimination.
Meanwhile, Hong Kong’s Kuan Yu-ching, Lorraine Wong, Fan Bo-yee and Valerie Cheng Hiu-wai exited in the round of 64.
The tournament attracted lots of world-class talent, including three-time Olympic champion and current world No 1 Lee Kiefer of the United States and world No 2 Martina Favaretto of Italy, both in action on Saturday.

data.title: Housing anxiety impeding Hong Kong fire trauma recovery: mental health experts
data.description:
Anxiety over housing has hindered the psychological healing of residents affected by the deadly Tai Po fire, mental health professionals have said, as Hong Kong authorities begin polling flat owners on their preferences for long-term resettlement.
The inferno that broke out on November 26 at Tai Po’s Wang Fuk Court claimed 161 lives, injured 79, and displaced almost 5,000 residents, most of whom are now living in hotels, youth hostels or transitional housing.
The government said on Friday that social workers had started polling flat owners on their long-term rehousing preferences, with subsidised homes in other districts and returning to the original site as early as 2035 after redevelopment, among nine options. Data collection is expected to finish by late January.
While the authorities have taken preliminary steps to address their housing concerns, experts warned that the insecurity posed challenges to psychological intervention, as most victims had little mental head space to deal with their trauma.
Opal Li Tin-yui, a clinical adviser to mental health charity Mind HK, said the group had assessed 120 Wang Fuk Court residents of the 600 living at five transitional housing projects operated by the Society for Community Organisation (SoCO).
She said that a few weeks ago, there was acute shock, disbelief and intense anxiety, but most residents were now “stable”, with their emotions numbed, set aside or suppressed.




“The majority appeared to be quite stable and quickly rebuilt their lives in the transitional homes, but that’s what they needed to do to keep their lives going,” she said.
“The first step to handle trauma is stability and safety … for them, this is simply not the right time to discuss the trauma because there is huge uncertainty about their future; it keeps them on edge.”
Li said most residents were concerned about whether they could stay in transitional housing permanently, and if not, when they needed to move again, especially those who had to make small renovations to their homes.
Nevertheless, many “pretended to be okay”, decorating their temporary homes for Christmas and maintaining daily routines, even as the pain of losing their flats remained visible, she said.
Some vulnerable residents, such as those with a psychiatric history or previous trauma, as well as elderly people with chronic conditions, had stronger reactions, including insomnia and unexplained pain.


She said the NGO’s current role was to identify the requirements of different survivors and provide psychological support after they had basic needs fulfilled.
“We are now at a critical juncture, the crisis phase is ending, but the real work of long-term recovery is just beginning.”
The charity hoped to establish different kinds of services tailored to the needs, trauma severity and openness of different groups, such as community-based intervention and trauma-informed sessions, as well as deliver services to frontline social workers, among others.
“What survivors are facing is not only the loss of homes, but also their identity, from an independent, self-reliant family to a ‘victim’ in need of assistance. There is a lot of work to do.”
Dr Catherine Li Ka-yan, a clinical psychologist with the Hong Kong Mental Health Association, which serves at least 30 Wang Fuk Court residents, noted a shift in the survivors’ psychological state as the disaster moved past the one-month mark.
“In the first two to three weeks, the residents were in survival mode, focusing mental resources on basic needs such as shelter, food and clothes, with little attention to their emotional needs,” Li said.
“But we have started to observe PTSD-like symptoms, such as flashbacks, nightmares and hypervigilance to the sound of fire alarms and sirens.”

She said that “avoidance behaviours” – a reluctance to return to Tai Po or discuss the fire – coupled with the general stigma surrounding mental health in Asian cultures, and the fact that residents were scattered across Hong Kong, had created hurdles for service delivery.
To bridge the gap, the NGO has turned to telehealth services, including Zoom and phone counselling, while social workers conduct in-person visits with those unable to commute.
She said that each person’s recovery progress was different and depended on the individual’s resilience and support system but early intervention was crucial.
“We have to acknowledge that much anxiety stemmed from uncertainties, as they kept asking how long they could stay in those shelters, when they need to move, and if they need to purchase the furniture all over again,” she said.
“If the authorities could pledge concrete, long-term financial and housing support to residents, it could greatly alleviate their anxiety.”
The Advisory Committee on Mental Health earlier announced support measures such as free grief counselling provided by seven NGOs, which has reached the families of 130 deceased.
The committee will also start a project in transitional housing where Wang Fuk Court residents are living to provide on-site mental health resources.
Separately, the 18111 mental health support hotline has received more than 700 calls and 50 messages related to the fire.
The Social Welfare Department, in response, said its social workers, psychologists and integrated community centre for mental wellness provided emotional support, but did not give service statistics.

data.title: Trump says protest-hit Iran in ‘big trouble,’ again threatens military strike
data.description:
US President Donald Trump said on Friday that Iran, faced with major protests, was in “big trouble” and again warned he could order military strikes.
“Iran’s in big trouble. It looks to me that the people are taking over certain cities that nobody thought were really possible just a few weeks ago,” Trump said.
Protests have taken place across Iran for 13 days in a movement sparked by anger over the rising cost of living that is now marked by calls for the end of the clerical system that has ruled Iran since the 1979 Islamic revolution that ousted the pro-Western shah.
Asked on his message to Iran’s leaders, Trump said, “You better not start shooting because we’ll start shooting too.”
“If they start killing people like they have in the past, we will get involved,” Trump said. “That doesn’t mean boots on the ground, but it means hitting them very, very hard where it hurts.”


Iran’s supreme leader Ayatollah Ali Khamenei vowed on Friday that the Islamic republic would not back down, as authorities pressed an internet blackout as part of a crackdown that has left dozens dead.
The biggest protests seen yet in the movement took place late on Thursday with large crowds marching through Tehran chanting slogans including “death to the dictator”.
Internet monitor Netblocks said authorities had now imposed a “nationwide internet shutdown” for the last 24 hours that was violating the rights of Iranians and “masking regime violence”.
In a separate statement, Amnesty International said the “blanket internet shutdown” aims to “hide the true extent of the grave human rights violations and crimes under international law they are carrying out to crush” the protests.
Norway-based NGO Iran Human Rights, raising a previous toll of 45 issued the day earlier, said at least 51 protesters, including nine children under the age of 18, have been killed by security forces and hundreds more injured.
The demonstrations represent one of the biggest challenges yet to the Islamic republic in its over 4½ decades of existence.
The protests late on Thursday were the biggest in Iran since 2022-2023 rallies nationwide sparked by the death in custody of Mahsa Amini after she was arrested for allegedly violating the Islamic republic’s strict dress code.
But Khamenei struck a defiant tone in his first comments on the escalating protests since January 3, calling the demonstrators “vandals” and “saboteurs”, in a speech broadcast on state television.
Khamenei said Trump’s hands were “stained with the blood of more than a thousand Iranians”, in apparent reference to Israel’s June war against the Islamic republic which the US supported and joined with strikes of its own.
He predicted the “arrogant” US leader would be “overthrown” like the imperial dynasty that ruled Iran up to the 1979 revolution.

“Last night in Tehran, a bunch of vandals came and destroyed a building that belongs to them to please the US president,” he said in an address to supporters, as men and women in the audience chanted the mantra of “death to America”.
“Everyone knows the Islamic republic came to power with the blood of hundreds of thousands of honourable people, it will not back down in the face of saboteurs.”
Trump said late Thursday that “enthusiasm to overturn that regime is incredible” and warned that if the Iranian authorities responded by killing protesters, “we’re going to hit them very hard. We’re ready to do it.”
In the Fox News interview late on Thursday, Trump said “enthusiasm to overturn that regime is incredible” and went as far as to suggest 86-year-old Khamenei may be looking to leave Iran.
“He’s looking to go someplace,” he said.
Iranian Foreign Minister Abbas Araghchi, on a visit to Lebanon, on Friday accused Washington and Israel of “directly intervening” to try to “transform the peaceful protests into divisive and violent ones”.
The son of the shah of Iran ousted by the 1979 Islamic Revolution, US-based Reza Pahlavi, urged Trump to intervene to help the protesters, adding “the people will be on the streets again in an hour”.
But judiciary chief Gholamhossein Mohseni Ejei warned that punishment of “rioters” would be “decisive, the maximum and without any legal leniency”.
Quoted by state television, he said a district prosecutor in the town of Esfarayen in eastern Iran and several members of the security forces had been killed late on Thursday in the protests.

The intelligence branch of the Revolutionary Guards, the security force entrusted with ensuring the preservation of the Islamic republic, said the “continuation of this situation is unacceptable” and protecting the revolution was its “red line”.
Meanwhile, Iranian state television on Friday broadcast images of thousands of people attending counter-protests and brandishing slogans in favour of the authorities in some Iranian cities.
The Haalvsh rights group, which focuses on the Baloch Sunni minority in the southeast, said security forces fired on protesters in Zahedan, the main city of Sistan-Baluchistan province, after Friday prayers, causing an unspecified number of casualties.
There were few videos emerging of other new protest actions late Friday, with some sources blaming this on the internet shutdown.
Amnesty International and Human Rights Watch said in a joint statement that since the start of the protests on December 28, security forces “have unlawfully used rifles, shotguns loaded with metal pellets, water cannon, tear gas and beatings to disperse, intimidate and punish largely peaceful protesters”.

data.title: How boredom can boost creativity and cognitive function, tackle anxiety and much more
data.description:
If I complained to my mother when I was a child that I was bored, she either told me to read a book or to make up a story to amuse myself.
“I’m bored” was a common refrain in children and teens in the past, agrees Michelle Kennedy, a postdoctoral research fellow at the University of the Sunshine Coast in Australia. In recent years, however, she says parents have scheduled their offspring’s time so tightly that there is no time for them to experience boredom.
Even when there is time, children today frequently have a device at hand to capture their interest.
They are on their phones rather than “pondering and wondering”, Kennedy says. They have developed a screen habit reinforced by parents who do the same now when they have time on their hands.

Clinical psychologist Dr Christian Chan, an associate professor in the department of psychology at the University of Hong Kong, agrees.
“We often escape boredom too quickly by turning to our phones,” Chan says. “Over time, we have learned to reach for them not only when we are bored, but even at the slightest hint of attention drifting.”
This automatic escape prevents us from facing boredom, Chan says. It also keeps us from important experiences that require patience, such as learning, creating or mastering a skill.
“These meaningful activities often feel boring at first, but they are exactly the ones that lead to growth.”
Chan has published several papers on boredom, including one entitled “Attention Drifting In and Out: The Boredom Feedback Model”. Its title summons memories of the child in me “gazing into space” – being afforded the time to daydream.
Chan says that while boredom is often viewed as a negative feeling, “it is more complex and meaningful than we think”.

Kennedy says a bit of boredom affords many benefits.
For a start, it allows our sympathetic nervous system to pause and reset – especially important in a world that is overstimulating. Constant stimulation can be a burden on our nervous system because it means we are always “on”. This is sometimes referred to as “allostatic overload”, and it can predispose us to anxiety.
So avoiding boredom can deprive us of a valuable and natural way to reset our sympathetic nervous system, she says. For some people, sitting with feelings can help manage anxiety – a condition that is on the rise across most age groups.
Boredom also allows us the opportunity to “flow” and develop our thoughts, which improves our creativity and problem-solving.
That space, Kennedy continues, also helps us develop more independent thinking, in which we seek alternative activities generated from our thought processes rather than relying on constant external input. It creates a pause and can encourage imagination and inventiveness.

Just as pain is the body’s way of letting us know that something is wrong and must be addressed, boredom plays a similar role.

If a child is bored at school, we need to learn why: are lessons too easy – or too hard?


If you’re bored in a relationship, you must ask whether it is the right one or if it needs some work.


If you are bored in your career or job, is there a way to change?


If you are bored with what you eat, can you get creative and cook something different or try a new restaurant?

Boredom is a useful way to gauge what is and is not working in your life and can be considered an opportunity, Chan says. It is a “decision point: should I keep doing what I am doing, or should I shift my attention to something more meaningful or rewarding?”
Being bored also comes with cognitive benefits, Kennedy says, specifically improvements in attention. That is because devices encourage the dopamine hit of continuous scrolling, where if what you view on the screen is not interesting, you quickly look at something else.
“In children and teens, when the brain is still developing in terms of connectivity and structural development, are we wiring attention networks to lose focus more quickly?”

Staying away from our screens for a bit also breaks that vicious loop of instant gratification, she says, something that drives compulsive device use.
Boredom also allows time to rest the brain and eyes, to nurture development in young brains. Brains that are still developing, Kennedy says, are vulnerable, so being constantly overactive or overstimulated can have negative impacts. Studies on eyes and postural development in youth have shown this, she adds.
Chan says boredom can be a tool to help us reflect and choose, and when we sit with it, we can endure and grow.
“Ask any star athlete or musician whether they have ever felt bored practising drills or scales. Without boredom, life might feel lighter and more entertaining, but it would likely be emptier as well.”
What we must learn to avoid is the knee-jerk, automatic reaction to escape – or what psychologists call “experiential avoidance”.
There is a valuable tip right there: missing out on boredom could mean missing out on unexpected experiences.
Like what you read? Follow SCMP Lifestyle on Facebook, X and Instagram. You can also sign up for our eNewsletter here.

data.title: Hong Kong’s new Legislative Council president must strike the right balance
data.description:
In a symbolic fresh start, the new Legislative Council has elected Starry Lee Wai-king as its president. The veteran lawmaker is experienced in rules and procedures and can preserve continuity while fostering change. Even so, the tasks ahead of her are daunting. How to cooperate closely with the executive branch and enhance “all-patriots” governance without compromising the constitutional functions of the legislature under the Basic Law will put her to the test.
The Democratic Alliance for the Betterment and Progress of Hong Kong (DAB) lawmaker defeated Ronick Chan Chun-ying by only five votes in the first contest between pro-government rivals for the post. Major parties reportedly allowed members a free vote in what was said to be in line with Beijing’s consent for a substantive race within the patriotic camp.
Lee is a safe pair of hands. As head of the House Committee in previous terms, she deputised for the president to chair meetings as needed. After becoming Hong Kong’s sole delegate to the National People’s Congress (NPC) Standing Committee in 2023, she stepped down as DAB party leader but continued her Legco duties.
Aware of concerns over her NPC standing committee role and duties, Lee said only four of its meetings would overlap with the Legco schedule each year. She stressed that her Legco work remained her primary focus and that she believed she could help Legco guide the authorities in integrating Hong Kong into the nation’s overall development. The dual roles need not run into conflict when Lee discharges her Legco duties according to the law. She also dismissed concerns that her presidency would lead to “one-party dominance”.
The Hong Kong and Macau Affairs Office hailed the selection of the Legco president as a success of the “patriots ruling Hong Kong” principle and a demonstration of the effectiveness of the new electoral system. The council will get down to business with a motion debate next week on relief work and reforms in the wake of the Tai Po fire tragedy that killed 161 people in November.
There is a perception among some people that the all-patriots Legco has swung from dysfunctional to little more than a rubber stamp. The Post has stressed the importance of the new Legco chief enhancing public confidence in the new governance approach and maintaining constructive working relations with the executive branch without compromising the duties of the council.
Hopefully Lee and the new legislature can rise to the challenge and strike a good balance between cooperating with the government under the executive-led approach while representing the voice of the public and performing its statutory functions to improve people’s livelihoods.

data.title: Regional cooperation led to arrest of alleged scam kingpin Chen Zhi
data.description:
Catching transnational criminals and neutralising their networks requires international cooperation. That may have been the lesson of Chen Zhi, whose alleged brazen criminal activities have shocked people and made global headlines. Now, the accused has been caught and extradited by Cambodia to China. Hong Kong authorities have frozen HK$2.75 billion (US$353 million) in assets belonging to Chen and his numerous businesses, but that was just the tip of the iceberg.
Chen and his alleged criminal enterprise have been accused of running scam centres in Cambodia and Myanmar by using fake adverts to lure jobseekers who were then confined in forced-labour camps to commit online fraud under threat of violence and torture. He was among three Chinese nationals extradited by Cambodia at China’s request. Phnom Penh said law enforcement from both countries had worked on the case – a welcome development.
Chen founded Prince Holding Group, which has been designated by the US Treasury Department as a “transnational criminal organisation”, and was allegedly involved in scams, telecoms fraud, human trafficking, torture, money laundering and illegal online gambling. The group has been described as one of Cambodia’s largest corporations, with companies spanning real estate, banking, finance, tourism, logistics, technology, food and beverages and lifestyle. It’s extraordinary that Chen and his cronies were able to conduct their alleged criminal empire for so long and on such a scale.
Besides being criminally indicted, Chen and others linked to the Prince Group have had their assets seized in the US, including US$14 billion worth of bitcoins. Singapore, South Korea and Taiwan have also seized assets; Britain has placed Chen under sanctions. An investigative task force was formed as early as 2020 in Beijing targeting Chen and his associates. But the cross-border nature of their alleged criminal enterprise required the cooperation of governments and parallel law enforcement agencies.
With some success, China has been pushing Southeast Asian countries to work together on regional crimes such as telecoms fraud. They have a shared interest in catching alleged transnational criminals like Chen and breaking up their networks.

data.title: US Commerce Department drops plan to crack down on Chinese drones
data.description:
The US Commerce Department ‍said on Friday it has withdrawn a plan to impose restrictions on Chinese drones to address national security concerns after an earlier crackdown on passenger cars and trucks.
Last month, the US Federal Communications Commission barred imports of new models of foreign-made drones and critical components including from China’s DJI and Autel on US national security ⁠grounds.
The FCC this week said it was exempting some non-Chinese drones from the restrictions.
The Commerce Department said in September it planned to issue rules that could restrict or potentially bar imports of Chinese drones to address information and communication technology supply chain issues.
It sent the proposal to the White House for review on October 8 before withdrawing the proposal on Thursday, according to a posting on a government website on Friday.


The ‍FCC restrictions mean Chinese dronemakers will not be able to obtain the necessary approval to sell new models of drones or critical components ‍in the US. They do not prohibit the import, sale or use of any existing drone models the agency previously authorised, nor do they impact any previously bought drones.
The White House and Commerce held meetings on ‌the drone proposal through December 19 and met with DJI officials on December 11, according to records posted online.
DJI told officials that imposing blanket restrictions ‍on drones manufactured in China would be “unnecessary, conceptually flawed, and would be extremely harmful to US stakeholders”.
The move comes as Washington has frozen some actions targeting China ahead of US President Donald Trump’s planned meeting with Chinese President Xi Jinping in April. A government official briefed on the matter said it appeared the decision to withdraw the drone rule was tied ‌to that ⁠effort.
Neither the Commerce Department nor DJI immediately responded to requests for comment.
The Commerce Department in January 2025 said it was seeking input on potential rules to safeguard the supply chain for drones, saying threats from China and Russia “may offer our adversaries the ability to remotely access and manipulate these devices, exposing sensitive US data”.

Commerce also said in September it was considering similar rules to address concerns about medium- and ‌heavy-duty truck imports after finalising rules in January 2025 under then-President Joe Biden that effectively bar nearly all Chinese cars and trucks from the US market over the same concerns.
The commercial truck proposal has not gone to the White House for ‌review.
Chinese imports account for the ‌vast majority of US commercial drone sales. More than half come from DJI, the world’s largest drone manufacturer.
The Commerce Department in January 2025 said it was considering restrictions on systems in imported drones like on-board computers, communications and flight control systems, ground control stations, operating software and data storage.
Some experts said restricting key systems could have led to an effective ban on Chinese drones operating in the United States.

data.title: Style according to … Hong Kong-born, London-based designer Robert Wun
data.description:
Hong Kong-born, London-based Robert Wun made history as the first Hong Kong designer to show at Paris Haute Couture Week back in 2023. Since then, his shows have become a hot ticket and a highlight of Paris Fashion Week. Wun’s dramatic gowns, often inspired by horror films, have attracted such A-list fans as Lady Gaga and Lisa from Blackpink. Here, the designer shares his favourite finds from around the world.
What I’m watching

I really enjoyed Dandadan, created by Japanese mangaka Yukinobu Tatsu. I love how modern anime is interpreted, and knowing the story behind the creator’s journey makes it extra special. I’m also looking forward to seeing [supernatural mystery horror film] Weapons in the cinema.
My go-to beauty products

Penhaligon’s Halfeti perfume is essential – one of the looks from my recent couture show was even named after it. I also enjoy Le Labo Rose 31.
Places I love in London

Victoria Park – I love going there with my dog as a break from the city. Koya Soho is my favourite udon place in London, and of course, my London studio – I do enjoy working and am excited to be in my workspace.
My favourite bling

My rings. Most of them are gifts from loved ones.
Where I love to stay

Carlyle & Co. hosted me the last time I was in Hong Kong, and their hospitality was top-notch. It was some of the best service I’ve received in a very long time.
Where I’d love to go next

Greece! I can’t believe I’ve never been. It’s at the top of my list, and I hope to visit within the next year or so.
My style icons

Tilda Swinton and Grace Jones. Both to me symbolise style before fashion, and dressing not to impress but to express. Also, I feel that their integrity and contributions in their fields have led to an incredible bond with the designers they work with – a magical chemistry.
A piece I’m proud of

The Purple Rain Bride from our latest couture show. As the last look I sketched out while we were making the collection, it fills in a very important part of the narrative that was missing and was needed to complete the collection. I knew the moment the sketch was done that this look was the one.

data.title: How PLA could use ‘decapitation’ strike to counter Taiwan’s ‘porcupine’ strategy
data.description:
Taiwan’s asymmetric warfare plan – also known as the “porcupine” strategy – could pose a significant threat to a PLA campaign against the island, a mainland Chinese military magazine has warned.
But the People’s Liberation Army could counter that strategy with an American-style “decapitation” strike, according to the article in the November issue of Naval and Merchant Ships.
It analysed how the porcupine strategy played out in Taiwan’s annual military exercise in July – known as Han Kuang – which was its biggest ever, and also looked at possible countermeasures the PLA could take.


The asymmetric warfare strategy would see Taiwan become like a porcupine – covered in “quills” such as lightweight, shoulder-launched air defence missiles to fend off the much larger and better armed PLA.
Influenced by US military advisers and backed by American arms sales, the strategy relies on deploying a vast number of small and low-cost combat platforms like FIM-92 Stinger portable air defence missiles and Switchblade 300 anti-tank loitering munitions, according to the article.
They would be used to launch “successive waves of attacks” on high-value targets like tanks, warships and radar systems in a war of attrition.
It said the aim was to turn the island into a fortress of layered defence, making use of the urban landscape and geography to drag the PLA into a protracted and costly conflict.
The article said Taiwan’s military was integrating new weapons – specifically Himars rocket systems and M1A2T tanks – with Taiwanese-made equipment like Land Sword II air defence missiles.
It also noted that urban warfare drills had been part of this year’s Han Kuang exercise.
“The threat is obvious,” the article said. “It reflects a strategic approach aimed first at stabilising the battlefield situation and then turning the tide in its favour.”
The United States last month approved a record US$11.1 billion weapons package for Taiwan that will boost its asymmetric capabilities with Himars and ATACMS missiles, Altius loitering munitions, Javelin and TOW anti-tank missiles, and tactical internet warfare capabilities.
The arms sale enraged Beijing and prompted the PLA to conduct large-scale live-fire drills around self-governed Taiwan last week.


Beijing sees Taiwan as part of China, to be reunited by force if necessary. It has ramped up pressure on Taiwan since pro-independence leader William Lai Ching-te took office in 2024.
Most countries, including the US, its main international partner, do not recognise Taiwan as an independent state. But Washington is opposed to any attempt to take the island by force and is legally bound to supply it with weapons to defend itself.
In the article, a multipronged response to the porcupine strategy was proposed. It said the PLA could bypass frontline resistance to directly target Taiwan’s political and military leadership in a “decapitation” strategy like the US military has used in previous campaigns.
The article used the example of the US-led invasion of Iraq in 2003, noting that instead of getting bogged down trying to seize every stronghold or city, the American military advanced straight to the capital, Baghdad, and captured Iraqi leader Saddam Hussain – which it said was a “classic example of urban warfare”.
Another “decapitation” operation was carried out on the Venezuelan leader last weekend when American special forces raided its capital, Caracas, and abducted Nicolas Maduro and his wife, taking them to New York to stand trial on multiple US criminal charges.
According to the article, the PLA could also use unmanned warfare to counter Taiwan’s porcupine strategy. It suggested AI-powered drones could be used for precision reconnaissance and firing guidance, while equipment such as unmanned tanks, armoured vehicles and robot dogs could be sent in to clear streets – potentially resulting in fewer casualties and collateral damage.
The article also highlighted the use of psychological and political warfare to encourage defections and surrender, drawing on examples from China’s civil war.
